teaching.title: Java Method Structure
teaching.analogy: A method is a named machine in a factory. You feed materials in (parameters), the machine processes them, and outputs a product (return value). The blueprint specifies what materials it accepts and what it produces.
teaching.content: Java method: accessModifier returnType methodName(params) { body }. void = no return. Must return declared type. static methods belong to the class (call without object). Instance methods belong to objects.

examples.title: Method Anatomy
examples.explanation: Every part of a Java method declaration.

examples.title: static vs Instance Methods
examples.explanation: static needs no object. Instance needs one.

teaching.funFact: Java's main method MUST be 'public static void main(String[] args)' — this exact signature is hard-wired into the JVM as the program entry point. One character wrong and your program won't run! ⚡

teaching.title: Pass-by-Value in Java
teaching.analogy: Java ALWAYS passes by value — when you hand someone a paper with the number 5, they can scribble on THEIR copy all they want, but your original paper stays unchanged. For objects, you pass a COPY of the reference (a copy of the address).
teaching.content: Java passes primitives by VALUE — the method gets a copy. Changing it doesn't affect the original. For objects, the REFERENCE is copied — the method can mutate the object's state but can't reassign the caller's variable.

examples.title: Pass-by-Value Demonstrated

teaching.funFact: Java is ALWAYS pass-by-value — even for objects! This confuses many developers. The 'value' passed for an object IS the reference (memory address), which is why objects appear to be pass-by-reference. They're not! 🤯

mcq.q: What does 'void' mean as a return type?
mcq.explanation: void: The method produces no return value. You cannot use void methods in expressions. Call them as standalone statements.

mcq.q: static method vs instance method — key difference?
options: static belongs to the class (no object needed); instance belongs to an object
static is public; instance is private
mcq.explanation: static: Belongs to the CLASS. Called as ClassName.method() or just method() within the class. Instance methods require an object: obj.method().
Pass By Value: Java ALWAYS passes primitives by value. The method receives a COPY. Changing the parameter inside the method doesn't affect the caller's variable.

mcq.q: method returns int but has 'return;' with no value — this is:
options: Compile error — must return an int value
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: If a method declares int return type, it MUST return an int value. A bare 'return;' or falling off the end without returning is a compile error.

mcq.q: Can Java methods have multiple return statements?
options: Yes — control flow determines which one executes
mcq.explanation: Multiple Returns: A method can have multiple return statements (typically in if/else branches). The first one reached ends the method.

coding.title: Return type for text
coding.objective: A method that returns text should have what return type?
coding.hint: Text in Java is the String class.

coding.title: void method
coding.objective: A method that only prints and returns nothing uses which keyword?
coding.hint: No value returned → void return type.

coding.title: Pass by value result
coding.objective: int x=5; doubleIt(x); If doubleIt sets param*=2, x after?
coding.hint: Java primitives are passed by value. x's copy is doubled, not x itself.

options: Compile error — not all code paths return a value. Add: return b; after the if.
debugging.answer: Compile error — not all code paths return a value. Add: return b; after the if.
debugging.hint: Java compiler ensures ALL code paths return. If a <= b, nothing is returned → compile error.

sublevels.title: OVERLOADING & RECURSION
sublevels.subtitle: Same name, different params; self-calling methods

teaching.title: Method Overloading
teaching.analogy: Overloading is like a restaurant with the same dish name on the menu but different sizes — 'pizza(small)', 'pizza(medium)', 'pizza(large)'. Same name, different specifications. Java picks the right version at compile time.
teaching.content: Method overloading: same name, different parameter list (different count or types). Return type alone cannot differentiate. Java resolves at compile time (static dispatch). Overloading ≠ overriding.

examples.title: Method Overloading

teaching.funFact: Java's println is massively overloaded! There are 10 versions: println(int), println(double), println(String), println(boolean), println(char[]), println(Object)... Java picks the right one at compile time! 🖨️

teaching.title: Recursion
teaching.analogy: Recursion is like looking up a word in a dictionary and the definition uses another word you don't know, so you look THAT up, and so on — until you find a word you already know (base case). Then you unwind all the lookups.
teaching.content: Recursion: method calls itself. MUST have a base case (stop condition) or it causes StackOverflowError. Recursive call should move toward the base case. Every recursive solution can be written iteratively (and vice versa).

examples.title: Factorial — Classic Recursion
Fibonacci — Two Recursive Calls
examples.explanation: Each call makes TWO more calls.

teaching.funFact: Java's default call stack depth is ~500-1000 method calls (depending on JVM settings). Recursion deeper than this causes StackOverflowError. This is why iterative solutions are preferred for large inputs! ⚠️

mcq.explanation: Parameter List: Overloaded methods must differ in parameter COUNT or TYPES (the signature). Return type alone doesn't differentiate — compile error if only return type differs.

mcq.q: What happens without a base case in recursion?
mcq.explanation: StackOverflowError: Without a base case, the method keeps calling itself indefinitely. The call stack fills up and JVM throws StackOverflowError.

mcq.q: factorial(4) with base case n<=1: return 1. Result?

options: Overloading is same class/different params; Overriding is subclass/same signature
Overloading is runtime; Overriding is compile-time
mcq.explanation: Key Difference: Overloading = same class, same name, different params (compile-time). Overriding = subclass replaces parent method with same signature (runtime polymorphism).

coding.title: Base case
coding.objective: static int fact(int n){if(n<=___) return 1; return n*fact(n-1);}
coding.hint: Base case: 1! = 1 and 0! = 1. Stop at n<=1.

coding.title: Factorial of 5
coding.starterCode:
// factorial: n<=1 returns 1, else n*factorial(n-1)
// factorial(5) = ___

coding.title: Overloading valid?
coding.objective: Two print(int x) methods in same class — valid overloading?
coding.hint: Identical signatures — compile error. Overloading needs different parameter types or count.

options: Missing base case — will StackOverflow. Add: if(n<=0) return 0;
debugging.answer: Missing base case — will StackOverflow. Add: if(n<=0) return 0;
debugging.hint: Every recursive method MUST have a base case to stop the recursion.

teaching.title: Varargs & Method Scope
teaching.analogy: Varargs is a method that accepts 'as many as you bring' — like a buffet that works for 1, 5, or 20 guests. Inside the method, varargs behaves as an array. Method scope: variables declared inside a method are local — born at declaration, die at the closing brace.
teaching.content: Varargs: returnType method(Type... name) — accepts any number of args, treated as array. Method scope: local variables don't exist outside the method. Parameters are also local. Calling convention: stack frame created on call, destroyed on return.

examples.title: Varargs
examples.explanation: Type... accepts any number of arguments.

teaching.funFact: Java's System.out.printf uses varargs internally! printf(String format, Object... args) — that's why you can pass any number of format arguments. Varargs was added in Java 5 alongside generics! 🖨️

mcq.q: int... nums in a method parameter — what is nums inside the method?
mcq.explanation: Array: Varargs ... treats the arguments as an array internally. You can use for-each, nums.length, nums[i] — all array operations work.

mcq.q: A variable declared inside a method exists:
mcq.explanation: Local Scope: Local variables live on the call stack frame. They're created when declared and destroyed when the method returns. Not accessible outside the method.

mcq.q: Can two different methods have a local variable with the same name?
options: Yes — they're separate in different stack frames
mcq.explanation: Separate Scopes: Each method has its own stack frame. Two methods can both have int i — they're completely independent variables in different scopes.

coding.title: Varargs syntax
coding.objective: What syntax makes a parameter accept variable number of int args?
coding.hint: Three dots ... after the type: int... nums

coding.title: Varargs call
coding.objective: sum(int... n). What does sum(1,2,3,4,5) return if it adds all?

options: result is local to compute() — doesn't exist in show(). Pass it as parameter or make it a field.
debugging.answer: result is local to compute() — doesn't exist in show(). Pass it as parameter or make it a field.
debugging.hint: Local variables die when the method returns. result doesn't exist outside compute().

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE CALL STACK
teaching.analogy: The Call Stack tracks every method call in your program — each call pushes a new frame, each return pops one. The Boss controls this stack. Prove your method knowledge is deep enough to collapse its structure!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 5 Java: method anatomy, void vs typed return, static vs instance, pass-by-value for primitives, method overloading (params, not return type), recursion base case, StackOverflow, varargs ..., local scope.

examples.title: Full Method Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ returnType methodName(params) { return; }
✅ void — no return value
✅ static — class method, no object
✅ primitives passed by VALUE
✅ Overloading: same name, diff params
✅ Recursion: base case REQUIRED
✅ StackOverflowError without base case
✅ int... varargs — any number
✅ Local vars die with the method

teaching.funFact: The call stack in Java is typically 256kb-512kb per thread. Each method call uses some of this. Recursive Fibonacci for fib(50) would need 2^50 frames — impossible! This is why memoization and dynamic programming exist. 🔢

mcq.q: Why can't you overload only by return type in Java?
options: Cannot — call site doesn't always use return value, so ambiguous
mcq.explanation: Ambiguous: int x = add(1,2) — compiler can determine return type. But add(1,2); alone can't determine which overload. Java requires parameter list difference.

mcq.q: static int fact(int n){if(n==1)return 1;return n*fact(n-1);} fact(3)=?
void method with no return — what happens if you write 'return x;'?
options: Compile error — void can't return a value
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: A void method cannot have 'return value;'. It CAN have bare 'return;' to exit early, but cannot return a value.

mcq.q: After calling doubleNum(int x){x*=2;} with int n=5. n=?
mcq.explanation: Unchanged: n is passed by value. The method gets a copy. n stays 5 in the caller.

mcq.q: sum(int... nums). What does sum(1,2,3) pass as nums?
mcq.explanation: Array: Varargs packs 1,2,3 into int[] {1,2,3}. Inside sum, nums is an int array you can iterate with for-each or access with nums[i].

coding.title: Boss: Method return
coding.objective: static ___ square(int n){return n*n;} What return type?
coding.hint: n*n where n is int → result is int.

debugging.brokenCode: static int add(int a, int b)
options: Compile error — cannot overload by return type alone. Both have (int,int) params.
debugging.answer: Compile error — cannot overload by return type alone. Both have (int,int) params.
debugging.hint: Overloading requires different parameter LISTS. Return type doesn't count. Both have (int,int) → duplicate method.
